summary: I was born and raised in Sichuan, China, a place famous for its spicy food. Growing up there shaped my love for bold flavors, and I was thrilled to find that Sichuan cuisine is popular even here in Minnesota. It’s a comforting connection to my roots amidst my life in the United States.

I attended the University of Minnesota for graduate school and earned my Ph.D. in Chemistry there. Choosing this university was a big decision since it meant moving to a new country and culture, but it turned out to be the right choice. It’s also where I met my husband, Aaron, which made my experience even more meaningful.

Aaron is my husband and a classical musician, which is quite different from my world of science. We married in 2013 after the Defense of Marriage Act was overturned, and we’ve shared many milestones together since then. Despite our different careers, we’ve built a life filled with love, two adorable cats, and a home in Minnesota.

Minnesota has been my home since I came here for graduate school. It’s a place known for its welcoming spirit, especially when it became the first state to defeat the 2012 amendment banning same-sex marriage. After years of living apart due to our jobs, Aaron and I finally settled back in Minnesota, where we enjoy our weekends and community.

In 2012, Minnesota was the first state to defeat Amendment 1, which aimed to ban same-sex marriage. Aaron and I witnessed this historic event together, and it was a pivotal moment for us as a couple. It gave us hope and momentum leading up to our own marriage the following year.
cues: defeat of ban on same-sex marriage

summary: The Defense of Marriage Act, or DOMA, was overturned by the Supreme Court, which was a huge victory for us. Aaron and I got married on the coldest day of 2013, shortly after this ruling, making our marriage legally recognized. This event marked a new chapter in our lives and in the fight for equality.

entity: Supreme Court of the United States
summary: The Supreme Court played a crucial role in our lives when it overturned the Defense of Marriage Act. This decision allowed Aaron and me to marry legally in 2013, which was a deeply meaningful moment for us. It felt like a turning point not just personally but for many couples across the country.

Aaron and I spent some time living apart due to our specialized careers, and Seattle was one of the cities where one of us worked. During those two years, we flew between cities like Seattle, the Quad Cities, Dallas, and Minneapolis to maintain our relationship. It was challenging but also showed our commitment to each other.

The Quad Cities was another city where either Aaron or I worked during the two years we lived apart. Traveling between places like the Quad Cities, Seattle, Dallas, and Minneapolis was tough but necessary for our careers. It made finally settling together in Minnesota all the more rewarding.

Dallas was one of the cities where Aaron or I worked during the period we lived apart. Managing our relationship across cities like Dallas, Seattle, and the Quad Cities was difficult, but it strengthened our bond. Eventually, we moved back together to Minnesota to build our life side by side.

Minneapolis was one of the cities we traveled to during the years Aaron and I worked apart. It’s also part of the greater Minnesota area where we finally reunited and settled down. Living in Minneapolis and Minnesota overall has given us a sense of home and community after years of moving around.
